title: Chugiak fisherman earns $37,243 in halibut derby
summary: Jerry Saunders of Chugiak picked up his check for $37,243 on Friday for winning the Homer Jackpot Halibut Derby. Saunders won with his huge 358.4-pound fish, landed June 28. Saunders' fish is the largest derby winner since 1997, when Jeffrey Council of Oregon won with a 359.3-pound halibut. But the prize money has continued a steady downward trend since 2004, when Don Hanks of Nevada won $51,298. The prize money is based on the number of derby tickets sold. There are a number of other categories in which anglers can earn money. Last year's winner, Duane Olson, took home $43,612.

title: Saunders' 358.4-pounder takes Homer halibut jackpot
summary: Photo courtesy of Homer Chamber of Commerce enlarge Jerry Saunders' winning fish of the Homer Jackpot Halibut Derby is the third largest in derby history at 358.4 pounds. The exact amount of the check Saunders will pocket Friday at the derby awards party is still being calculated. Last year, the winner-take-all jackpot was more than $42,000. title: Heavy rain, horse traffic along Indian Creek path breeds mess
summary: I NDIAN VALLEY TRAIL -- Pieces of pressure-treated two-by-six that once served to plank boardwalks across wetlands here now litter the big mudholes dotting this popular trail south of Anchorage. Most of the bridges across little creeks, meanwhile, are in shambles. Between them, the surface of the trail that winds through the spruce and hemlock forest on its way toward the south entrance to Ship Creek Valley in the Chugach State Park is largely muddy goo. The good news is that winter will soon hide most of the damage beneath a blanket of snow. Next year, say park officials, who knows? Some damage may heal itself. Some will certainly need repair. What caused the damage is, however, obvious to anyone who ventures onto the trail. This is a man-made mess. Or should that be horse-made mess? Take…

title: Kenai woman's fowl fever becomes duck addiction
summary: KENAI -- Hunting means different things to different people. To some it is a way to save on food bills by filling the freezer with wild game. For others it is an excuse to fraternize with friends. And it can offer a way for us to reconnect with the past, when hunting was more essential to survival. But for one young women from Soldotna, hunting is mostly about one thing: escape. "It's a nice trade after sitting in the office all day," said Christine Cunningham, a self-professed "duck-a-holic." At first glance, one might never guess this sharply dressed 29-year-old with tanned skin, frizzy blond hair and perfect makeup would enjoy firing a shotgun at fleeing fowl during the first light of the day. However, after pushing papers all day as a judicial assistant at the Kenai courthouse, Cunning…

title: Hunters caught on horns of antlers dilemma
summary: Everything might have turned out differently, and better for all, if just one of the Haines hunters had muttered the words "not guilty, your honor.'' Instead, they bowed their heads, went along with an Alaska state trooper's interpretation of a new state regulation aimed to prevent the alteration of antlers, accepted the letter of the law and told a magistrate to judge them guilty. Lost in the process was the intent of the regulation, and now the community of Haines is in something of an uproar that threatens to spread statewide. At issue is what would at first appear a simple rule designed to stop moose hunters from altering big antlers to make them look like small antlers. A little history is in order here. Moose hunting in most of Alaska these days is governed by a system designed to pr…

title: Bogs frozen enough  to ride on
summary: C aught between the seasons? Not enough snow to ski. No salmon left to hook. Too warm to skate the icy expanses of Potter Marsh. Too cold to think about getting out the boat. OK, maybe it isn't quite that bad. If you've got some old cross-country boards you're willing to sacrifice to the rocks of Powerline Pass, you can ski. You can also make the long drive to Hatcher Pass. Sixty-five to 70 miles really isn't long, although in the dim light of the short days of November it seems twice as long as the same distance in summer. There are still some salmon coming back too. A handful of fresh silvers will trickle into the Kenai River through November. Of course, the Alaska Department of Fish and Game now orders the salmon season closed at the end of October. But, hey, there are still the steelhe…

title: Snowmachiner dies in collision with well casing
summary: A Houston man died Sunday after his snowmachine collided with a well casing, according to Alaska State Troopers. Dewey J. Staloch, 61, was declared dead at Mat-Su Regional Medical Center, where he was taken after the accident on Calder Road in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough east of Wasilla. Troopers responded at 5:50 p.m. to a snowmachine collision to find Staloch, who was riding a 1993 Arctic Cat, had struck a metal well casing in the yard. He was not wearing a helmet, and alcohol was not a factor in the collision, according to troopers. In November 2004, Staloch survived a frozen night in the forest after falling into the Nelchina River while hunting with a friend. His hunting companion, Linda Kakaruk, shot and wounded a caribou near Mile 144.5 Glenn Highway, and together they tracked the…
